query: mortality rates following emergency general surgery laparotomies

retrieved_docs.title: Large Bowel Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The optimal surgical approach for malignant obstruction remains a topic of debate, both in curative and palliative settings, for elective and emergent situations. Significant discussion persists regarding single- versus two-stage procedures for left-sided obstruction. Many stomas do not get reversed, and individuals who undergo reversal are subject to possible increased morbidity and mortality, with uncertain long-term survival benefits. Age, ASA status, operative conditions, cancer stage, and surgeon experience all influence outcomes. The use of tube decompression for left-sided malignant obstruction as a bridge to surgery remains debated, suggesting that palliative stenting is preferable to a colostomy due to lower morbidity and mortality. Studies comparing colostomy to stenting found similar morbidity but shorter recovery for stenting, but stent migration and perforations increased morbidity and mortality. Stent complications may be associated with the use of drugs such as bevacizumab, an antiangiogenic. Stenting may increase the potential for a successful subsequent laparoscopic tumor resection but may promote tumor dissemination and lymph node invasion and pose an increased risk for perforation, both of which impact disease-free survival. However, many studies comparing stenting to emergency surgery found no difference in recurrence, 3-year, or 5-year mortality. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Strangulated Hernia(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Overall mortality and prognosis are influenced by whether the surgery was emergent or elective. For elective surgical repair of a groin hernia, the risk of mortality is low at 0.1%. Mortality is increased to up to 3% with emergency surgery for strangulated bowel, notably when bowel resection is performed. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Large Bowel Obstruction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency intervention for malignant obstruction reduces long-term survival, and obstructed malignancy is associated with worse overall survival compared to non-obstructed malignancy. [6] Reported mortality for emergency surgery for obstructed colorectal cancer is greater than 11%. Factors associated with a poor outcome include age older than 70, right-sided obstruction, a significant departure from ideal body weight, sepsis, and elevated creatinine. Chronic diseases, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and diabetes, are associated with poor prognoses. [5] In a study involving 30,000 patients admitted with large bowel obstruction, those with a benign process had the highest inpatient mortality. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Room Thoracotomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Over the last 40 years, the indications for emergent thoracotomy have been refined in response to improved data and results. Currently, in the right population, this procedure has shown excellent results in the mortality reduction in specific circumstances. Overall, however, morbidity and mortality are high, with a survival rate ranging from 7.4 to 8.5 percent depending on the mechanism of injury. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Clinical Significance -- Mortality
retrieved_docs.content: Many different attempts and strategies have been undertaken to quantify morbidity and mortality risks in older patients in previous years. Most have discovered age to be an independent risk factor, along with the invasiveness of the surgery and baseline comorbidities. For outpatient surgery, a Medicare database review discovered a mortality rate of 0.23% on the day of surgery and a 0.66% 30-day mortality rate, with higher risk associated with ages over 85. For inpatient noncardiac surgery within the Veteran Affairs system, patients over the age of 80 had an 8% 30-day mortality risk, with a 5% increase in relative risk for each year over 80. Additional mortality risk factors include diagnosis of ischemic or nonischemic heart failure, emergency surgery, invasive surgeries (abdominal surgery, aortic aneurysm repair, and thoracic surgery), and delay in emergent surgical intervention. Even minor procedures such as colonoscopies and cataract surgeries place patients at significant risks, and thorough efforts should be taken to minimize complications. [72] [1] [73] [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Calculation of an operative risk is not sufficient for shared decision making. Prediction of the potential benefit of an intervention is another important part of the risk/benefit ratio estimation. The risk of mortality of 10% is inappropriately high for any patient undergoing an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy and does not favor surgical treatment. However, mortality as high as 70% may still be acceptable for a patient with multiple comorbidities and a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. The potential benefit from surgery is better estimated based on the published data on immediate and long-term results or surgery department audit and evaluation reports. The surgeon’s personal experience is also important to consider, but it is biased by definition unless the outcomes were verified within a formal research project. The art of medicine, however, will always play an important role in decision making, especially in complex cases. Despite being subjective, the physician’s feeling of a possible unfavorable outcome has a reasonable predictive value. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Large Bowel Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The World Journal of Emergency Surgery (WJES) treatment guidelines for perforated colon cancer, if the perforation is at the tumor site, recommend resection with anastomosis for a right- or left-sided process if there is no sepsis. If left-sided cancer perforates at a site proximal to the tumor, a subtotal colectomy may be required. In this setting, the recommendation is, if possible, limited resection of the terminal ileum and allowance for a distal colon remnant above peritoneal reflection to reduce the impact of diarrhea and stool incontinence. No randomized controlled studies exist that compare the creation of an anastomosis versus a diverting stoma in the perforation setting, and an overlap in the percentage of anastomotic leaks is present when comparing emergent and elective cases. The WJES supports resection with anastomosis for right-sided obstructing cancers and an internal bypass versus stenting for nonresectable tumors and palliative situations. An option for right-sided stenting includes using a self-expanding through-the-scope stent. Neoadjuvant therapy is the standard of care for locally advanced rectal cancer, and a transverse colostomy may be created in this setting. The WJES asserts that stool in the colon does not affect the rate of anastomotic dehiscence. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cecal Volvulus -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There are only small case series and isolated reports on outcomes of patients managed with cecal volvulus. For those who have delayed diagnosis, the outcomes are poor. Even those who undergo timely surgery have high morbidity as a consequence of their age. Laparoscopic surgery is preferred to open surgery, but sometimes the urgency of the situation may not allow it. No matter how one approaches the patient, the family must be fully informed of the potential complications, including recurrence and a stoma. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Laparotomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The indications for a laparotomy have significantly reduced in recent times since the advent of minimal access surgery. It is however important to note that access is key to any surgical procedure. In many situations, keyhole surgeries may be extremely difficult or even impossible as the risk of injuries to vital structures may outweigh the benefits of minimal access procedures. In these situations, whether emergency or elective, the more traditional laparotomy remains the mainstay. Broadly, these situations include multiple dense adhesions from previous surgeries or inflammatory conditions, grossly distended intestines in intestinal obstruction or massive ascites in patients with end-stage liver or cardiac disease. Emergency conditions, such as those for acute intraperitoneal bleeding, uncontrollable gastrointestinal bleeding, blunt or penetrating abdominal injuries, generalised intraperitoneal sepsis due to perforated gastrointestinal tract are still the most common indications for laparotomy [3] . Elective procedures that involve a large specimen, such as Pancreaticoduodenectomy, pancreatic or intestinal transplants are also indications for laparotomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Bochdalek Hernia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Overall survival rates vary across the literature, with most population-based studies suggesting an overall mortality rate between 42% and 68%. [2] However, single-institution studies suggest a survival rate of upwards of 90%. [15] This difference in survival rates is sometimes attributed to 'hidden mortality,' which considers the intrauterine deaths before arrival at the reporting institution. Some research suggests there has been an overall increase in patient survival. This is thought secondary to advances in critical care. [16]

retrieved_docs.content: Trauma and hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: Laparotomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The term laparotomy implies a potentially major operation in most surgical circles, however, does not stipulate in the name what operation the patient will have after the major open incision is performed. With advances in minimally invasive surgery and robotics, an open cut is being utilized less often unless clinically indicated, such as in an emergency situation where access into the abdomen needs to be fairly rapid or where the intra-abdominal environment is not amenable to other techniques. [6] Thus it is imperative that the patient is appropriately resuscitated, electrolytes, and sepsis are managed, and that care is taken when making the surgical cut to minimize the insult on the patient’s physiology. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Appendectomy -- Complications -- Mortality
retrieved_docs.content: The mortality associated with appendectomy is low; it is considered a relatively safe procedure. Morbidity and mortality after appendiceal perforation are 5.1 per 1000. However, the mortality rate can differ depending on the geographic location, as evidenced by more developed countries having a rate of 0.09% to 0.24% and developing countries having a rate of 1% to 4%.

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: It has been estimated that about 234 million major surgical procedures are performed every year worldwide. [7] Any surgical procedure can lead to complications. The average mortality associated with inpatient surgery fluctuates from 0.5% to 7%. [8] [9] [10] [11] [12] Approximately 2 to 3 out of 10 patients develop complications after an elective surgical procedure. [9] [12] For the most common surgical procedures, some of the postoperative complications have been well recognized and accepted by the professional society as an unavoidable risk due to the nature of the disease and patient-related factors. For example, the long-term recurrence rate following inguinal hernia repair has been consistently reported as around 9% to 12%. [13] It seems impossible to reliably reduce this rate without comprehensive research projects and further development in the field. For some other surgeries, the risk of selected complications has been successfully reduced to minimal and currently reflects random and unpredictable events. A good example may be a common bile duct injury during laparoscopic cholecystectomy with a stable incidence of 0.1%. [14] [15] In many cases, postoperative complications are potentially preventable. It is a surgeon’s responsibility to evaluate the operative risk and provide patients with comprehensive counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: Volvulus -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Any delay in the diagnosis of cecal or sigmoid volvulus can be associated with high morbidity and mortality. Mortality rates appear to be much higher for cecal volvulus than sigmoid volvulus. When volvulus is treated non-surgically, recurrence rates are very high, approaching 40% to 60%. When surgery is done in unstable patients, mortality rates of 12% to 25% have been reported.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 666
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES includes Surgical incisions as a subcategory, indicating that the practice of making incisions is a fundamental component of operative and anesthetic care. Surgical incisions are clinically linked to several serious postoperative complications. Specifically, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are both associated with the presence of surgical incisions, reflecting the risk of herniation and bowel compromise after incision closure. In addition, Surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑described conditions فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (a strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (an incarcerated abdominal hernia), further emphasizing that improper healing or technical issues with incisions can lead to hernias that become trapped or strangulated, requiring prompt medical attention. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate, monitor for, and manage incision‑related hernia complications to improve surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 701
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions serve as a central concept linking a range of operative and postoperative conditions. Within the broader domain of OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES, surgical incisions are directly associated with several specific forms of incisional hernia. These include Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction, each representing a distinct clinical scenario in which the integrity of the abdominal wall is compromised after surgery. In addition to these English‑language descriptors, surgical incisions are also connected to Arabic‑language entities that describe similar hernia complications: فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia) and فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (strangulated incisional hernia). Furthermore, surgical incisions have a noted relationship with بضع ال رحم, a term referring to uterine prolapse, indicating that incision‑related factors may influence or be relevant to pelvic organ support. Together, these associations underscore the importance of careful incision planning, vigilant postoperative monitoring, and comprehensive management of hernia‑related risks to optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 794
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions are linked to several specific incisional hernia presentations, including Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction. These conditions illustrate how disruption of the abdominal wall after an incision can lead to obstruction or strangulation of bowel contents. In addition, Surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑described entities بضع ال رحم and شق البطن, which refer to uterine suspension and abdominal splitting procedures, respectively. The same category is also associated with فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia), underscoring the risk of compromised blood flow and entrapment of viscera following an incision. Moreover, the hierarchical relationship places OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES as a child of Surgical incisions, indicating that operative and anesthetic management are integral components of the care pathway for patients undergoing incisional procedures. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians anticipate complications, select appropriate surgical techniques, and coordinate peri‑operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions are closely linked to several forms of incisional hernia, including incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction. In addition to these hernia complications, surgical incisions have a relationship with بضع الرحم and are also associated with فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (incarcerated inguinal hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia). Recognizing these associations is important for surgical planning, postoperative monitoring, and timely management of hernia-related obstruction, gangrene, or other abdominal wall complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2483
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions are closely linked to several specific hernia conditions. Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene and Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene are both associated with Surgical incisions, reflecting the potential for postoperative complications that involve bowel obstruction and, in the latter case, gangrene. In addition, Surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑language descriptors فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia), indicating that similar pathological processes are recognized across linguistic contexts. Moreover, the broader category OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES is identified as a child of Surgical incisions, situating these incision‑related hernia risks within the larger framework of operative and anesthetic care. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians anticipate, diagnose, and manage hernia complications that may arise from surgical incisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1942
retrieved_docs.content: Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are all conditions that are associated with surgical incisions. Surgical incisions are likewise linked to the Arabic‑named entities فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (a strangulated inguinal hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (an incarcerated abdominal hernia). Within the broader classification of medical procedures, the category OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES includes surgical incisions as a subordinate concept, highlighting the central role of incisions in both operative technique and the development of various hernia complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 998
retrieved_docs.content: Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are all clinical entities that are closely linked to the concept of surgical incisions, indicating that these hernias typically arise as complications of abdominal wall cuts made during operative procedures. Surgical incisions serve as the overarching category that encompasses these specific hernia presentations and also includes the Arabic‑language descriptors شق البطن, فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق, and فَتْكٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس, each of which refers to various forms of abdominal or ventral hernia. Within the hierarchical structure of medical procedures, the broader classification “OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES” is identified as a child of surgical incisions, reflecting that all operative and anesthetic interventions are performed through or involve such incisions. Recognizing the relationship between surgical incisions and these hernia types, as well as the terminology used in different languages, is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate surgical planning, and effective patient counseling regarding the risks and management of incisional hernias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1849
retrieved_docs.content: Abdomen destructive procedure has parent classification of excision; lysis peritoneal adhesies, indicating that this surgical intervention is categorized under procedures that involve tissue excision and the lysis of peritoneal adhesions within the abdominal cavity. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1266
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Terminology incorporates “exploratory laparotomy” as a concept that is included in a specific subset, indicating that this surgical procedure is formally recognized within the standardized data model. In clinical contexts, “exploratory laparotomy” frequently co‑occurs with “ثقب معوي” (intestinal perforation), reflecting the common association of this operative intervention with the presence of a perforated bowel. Recognizing both the inclusion of exploratory laparotomy in the CDISC SDTM subset and its typical co‑occurrence with intestinal perforation supports accurate data capture, facilitates interoperability of clinical trial information, and informs clinicians about the likely underlying pathology when this surgery is performed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 895
retrieved_docs.content: Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are all clinical entities that are associated with surgical incisions. Surgical incisions are likewise associated with the Arabic‑language terms فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس, which describe strangulated or incarcerated hernias of the abdominal wall. Together, these relationships emphasize that various forms of incisional hernia—particularly those complicated by obstruction or gangrene—commonly arise in the setting of prior surgical incisions, and that the Arabic descriptors correspond to comparable hernia presentations in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 770
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions are central to operative care and are classified under the broader category of OPERATIONS AND ANSTHESIA PROCEDURES. Complications associated with surgical incisions include various forms of incisional hernia. Specifically, an Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene and a Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are both linked to surgical incisions, highlighting the risk of obstructive herniation after surgery. In addition, surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑described conditions فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia), underscoring the potential for both strangulation and incarceration of hernias at incision sites. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in anticipating, diagnosing, and managing postoperative hernia complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 5 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier designates “mortalita” (mortality) as an admissible descriptor for several clinical and procedural contexts. In this framework, mortality is recognized as a relevant outcome for a حادث (accident), for diagnostic procedures on the ear, for ileostomy and other enterostomy surgeries, for صورة المفصل الشعاعية (radiographic imaging of a joint), and for تنظير الجهاز الهضمي (gastrointestinal endoscopy). By permitting mortality to qualify these diverse events and interventions, the classification underscores the importance of considering death risk in accident management, ear diagnostic work‑ups, intestinal surgical procedures, joint radiography, and gastrointestinal endoscopic examinations, thereby supporting comprehensive risk assessment and informed patient care.
